<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: living-book-methodology
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/living-book-methodology.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/living-book-methodology.qmd@af85e47e6
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Living Book Methodology

A book about governed self-improvement has to be careful about how it
improves.

If the manuscript quietly rewrites old claims, forgets why a source was added,
renumbers chapters by hand, loses proof targets, hides failed validations, or
publishes reader editions without saying what was stripped away, then the book
has already contradicted its own architecture. It would be asking future AI
systems to preserve provenance, authority, residuals, and rollback while its
own evidence trail dissolved into ordinary editorial drift.

The living-book method is the discipline that prevents that. It treats the
book as a governed artifact graph that happens to render as prose. The graph
contains the manifest, outline, source inventory, source notes, claim matrix,
proof manifest, schemas, tests, changelog, release records, reader projections,
and public site. The prose is what readers see first. The graph is what lets
readers, reviewers, and future agents see how the prose is allowed to change.

This does not make the thesis true. It makes the state of the thesis
inspectable.

That distinction matters. A rendered website, a clean validator run, a release
record, or a polished reader chapter can show publication discipline. It cannot
prove the ASI Stack works. The living book earns trust by making that boundary
hard to miss.

Together with Evidence States and Claim Discipline, this chapter is the book's
methodological center. Evidence States controls how confident a claim is
allowed to sound. Living Book Methodology controls the source graph, proof
graph, release graph, reader projections, and revision receipts that keep that
confidence from drifting.

![Draft living book release pipeline.](../../../../assets/diagrams/living-book-release-pipeline.svg){#reader-fig-living-book-release-pipeline fig-alt="Draft living book release pipeline figure showing live book source, validation gates, reader derivatives, evidence records, format artifacts, and release records while keeping their authority boundaries separate."}

Figure boundary: this draft reader aid shows the living-book release pipeline.
It is not release-reviewed art and does not prove reader release approval,
ebook/PDF/DOCX/audio quality, source adequacy, or support-state promotion.

## Problem

The book itself must remain a living technical system rather than a static
anthology.

The architecture is too broad, too source-dependent, and too update-sensitive
for a one-time manuscript. New AI papers will arrive. Existing papers will
need better external grounding. Project Theseus and Circle evidence lanes may
become cleaner, or may reveal that a claim should stay weaker. Lean modules may
move from field projections to real transition predicates. Some chapters may
split. Others may merge because the current shape repeats the same skeleton
around adjacent ideas.

Without a living method, every improvement creates a new risk. A stronger
paragraph might hide that the support state did not move. A new citation might
enter prose before a source note exists. A chapter merge might lose a proof
hook. A reader edition might sound more certain than the live AI/research
chapter. A future writing agent might add a chapter and force every downstream
number, cross-reference, and release note to be repaired by hand.

The problem is not that the book changes. The problem is ungoverned change.

## Why Static Books Are Not Enough

Static books are good at freezing a polished argument. They are bad at showing
how an argument earned, lost, or failed to earn support.

A PDF can be beautiful and still hide the difference between a conjecture, a
source-derived comparison, a locally replayed fixture, a Lean theorem, and a
failed experiment. A wiki can change quickly and still lose claim identity. A
repository can run tests and still fail to tell humans what those tests mean.
A chat transcript can preserve author intent and still be unusable as public
evidence.

The ASI Stack needs all of those virtues without accepting their failure
modes. It needs readable prose, stable public structure, source provenance,
validation, proof hooks, version history, and explicit non-claims.

External governance and evaluation work raises the same pressure from another
direction. Risk-management frameworks, frontier-governance literature,
transparent benchmark reporting, living benchmark practice, and contamination
analysis all point toward lifecycle accountability. They do not validate this
book's claims. They do show why a serious technical argument should expose its
evidence state instead of treating publication as proof.

There is also a technical-publishing lineage. Literate programming joined
human explanation to executable structure. Jupyter Book and related
executable-book tools joined notebooks, Markdown, code execution, cross
references, and web publication. Quarto Books supplies this project's concrete
multi-chapter publishing substrate. The ASI Stack method builds on that
lineage, then adds claim states, source queues, proof manifests, release
records, reader/audio derivative boundaries, and explicit non-claims.

The audience split makes the requirement stricter. AI systems need the
structured scaffold. Researchers need sources, claim states, proof targets, and
evidence ledgers. Interested human readers need coherent prose that does not
ask them to wade through the same machinery on every page. A living book has
to serve all three without letting three separate manuscripts drift into three
separate truths.

## Core Claim

The living book should model the ASI Stack discipline through manifest-driven
Quarto, source queues, claim/evidence matrices, proof manifests, tests,
changelogs, and releases.

In the live AI/research book, that remains a design rationale at `argument`
support. The repository demonstrates parts of the method through generated
scaffolds, validators, proof manifests, schema fixtures, changelog entries,
release records, commits, and GitHub Pages publication. Those artifacts support
release discipline. They do not prove that every chapter is correct, that every
source has been interpreted perfectly, that the human-reader book is finished,
or that any ASI implementation is validated.

There is one narrow non-core evidence transition attached to this chapter's
methodology lane: the Phase 5 harness registry runner is recorded as
`synthetic-test-backed`. That status belongs to a specific registry-runner
claim, not to the chapter core claim and not to the ASI Stack as a whole.

The claim is modest on purpose. It says that a book about governed intelligence
should itself be governed.

## The Living Method

The method starts with stable identity.

`book_structure.json` controls the order of parts, chapters, files, and
appendices. That means chapters can move, split, merge, or be inserted without
manual renumbering becoming part of the intellectual task. `docs/book_outline.md`
controls chapter jobs, source queues, proof scope, mechanisms, failure modes,
and future writing targets. Source notes record what has actually been read.
Appendix C records claim labels and support states. The proof manifest records
formalization targets. The changelog records meaningful movement.

The separation is what makes later automation possible. A future agent should
not need private memory to know what to load, what changed, which source is
allowed to support which claim, or which validation command must run before
publication. The repository should tell it.

That is why the method is more than "write the book in Quarto." Quarto renders
the artifact. The living-book method governs how the artifact may change and
which evidence, review, release, or reader state each change is allowed to
claim.

The source discipline is equally important. Corben-authored papers, local
projects, external literature, conversation-mined packets, Google Drive
documents, and blocked sources do not have the same evidential role.
Conversation-derived material can preserve author intent, terminology,
lineage, and recovery tasks. It is not external evidence. Source-reported
benchmarks stay source-reported until artifacts are imported or reproduced.
External literature must have inventory records and source notes before it
becomes chapter prose or support language.

The same discipline applies to edition work. The live book remains the
canonical AI/research surface. Human view, curated reader chapters, EPUB, PDF,
DOCX, and audio scripts are projections from a validated live state. They may
change pacing, examples, openings, closings, and section flow. They may not
change claim meaning, support states, source boundaries, proof status, test
status, implementation horizons, or release artifact existence.

The human-readable book can diverge in voice. It cannot diverge in truth.

## Change Packets

The unit of living-book progress is a change packet.

A useful change packet says which chapter IDs changed, which source IDs are
involved, which claim IDs or support states were touched, which proof tags,
schemas, fixtures, or release records moved, which validation commands ran,
which render target was checked, which audience profile was affected, and what
the change does not imply.

Most of that packet is distributed across ordinary repository surfaces: the
diff, changelog, source notes, Appendix C, proof manifest, release records, and
validation output. The point is not to create paperwork for its own sake. The
point is to make revision auditable after the author, editor, or AI assistant
has moved on.

Benchmaxxing gives the book a ratchet metaphor: progress should leave a harder
frontier, a preserved regression floor, and visible residuals. VIEA gives it
artifact discipline: important outputs become durable records, not just useful
responses. Cognitive Loop Closure explains why repeated maintenance should
become scripts, validators, or reusable procedures. BugBrain is useful here as
a cautionary lineage source: explicit build and test workflows matter, but
speculative claims stay speculative. MoECOT and Road To AGI contribute runtime
and remaining-work discipline while remaining source-reported until public-safe
artifacts are imported or replayed.

The living-book method borrows that discipline for writing.

## Operating Loop

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Idea["New source, result, critique, or idea"] --> Triage["Triage source role and chapter ownership"]
  Triage --> Notes["Source note, blocker, or author-intent record"]
  Notes --> Draft["Live chapter or outline revision"]
  Draft --> Claims["Claim and evidence matrix check"]
  Claims --> Proofs["Proof, schema, fixture, or no-promotion blocker"]
  Proofs --> Validate["Validation and Quarto render"]
  Validate --> Release["Changelog, commit, release record, public site"]
  Release --> Reader["Human view or reader derivative"]
  Reader --> Residuals["Residuals, review notes, and next backlog"]
  Residuals --> Triage

  Notes -. "no note, no source-derived support" .-> Claims
  Proofs -. "no run, no result" .-> Claims
  Reader -. "projection, not equal authority" .-> Claims
  Validate -. "failure blocks publication" .-> Draft
```

The loop is a publication ratchet. It starts with a new input and ends with
residuals that become the next input. The dashed edges are the important part:
no note means no source-derived support, no run means no result, a reader
projection is not equal authority, and a failed validation blocks publication.

This is also how the book becomes easier to maintain over time. Repeated
manual work should become a script, schema, validator, proof target, checklist,
or release record. But closure has to be earned. A workflow should not become
automation until its inputs, outputs, failure behavior, and review path are
clear enough to preserve the evidence boundary.

## Interfaces And Invariants

The methodology has simple interfaces.

Source ingestion feeds source notes. Drafting feeds the claim matrix. Tests,
proofs, and replayable fixtures feed support-state decisions only through
accepted evidence-transition records. Release records feed the public site and
major-version publication history. Reader and audio workspaces derive from the
live source, then record what was stripped, rewritten, reviewed, or left
blocked.

The invariants are stricter than the interfaces:

- no fabricated source content;
- no fabricated test results;
- no hidden support-state movement;
- deprecated or refuted claims remain visible;
- stable IDs preserve lineage across chapter splits, merges, moves, proof
  target changes, and releases;
- release records state what was not reviewed, rendered, proved, tested, or
  packaged.

The hardest invariant is evidence separation. A source note, a schema check, a
Lean build, a Quarto render, a GitHub Pages deployment, a reader-artifact
review, and a human editorial judgment are different artifacts. They may all
matter. They cannot collapse into one vague word like "validated."

## Failure Modes

The living-book layer fails when publication activity is laundered into
evidence.

A polished chapter can make an unsupported idea feel inevitable. A successful
render can make a weak citation feel safe. A generated EPUB can make an
unreviewed reader edition feel released. A passing schema can make a protocol
record look operational. A green Pages run can make a reader forget that all
chapter core claims may still be at `argument`.

The method has to fight that failure directly. The public entry surface should
say what is evidenced and what is not. Appendix C should keep core support
states visible. The non-core evidence ledger should surface narrow wins without
promoting chapter cores. The roadmap should record blockers, demotions, and
negative outcomes instead of hiding them behind optimistic prose.

Another failure is automation drift. A validator can keep passing after the
writing contract has changed. That is why the book needs reviews of the
validators themselves, not only more validators. A living system is not
trustworthy because it has scripts. It is trustworthy when its scripts still
enforce the contract readers are relying on.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is already visible in the public Quarto
repository: manifest-driven book order, stable chapter IDs, source inventory,
source notes, claim/evidence matrix, proof manifest, validation scripts,
reader-overlay machinery, release records, Git history, and GitHub Pages.

That is enough to start the living-book method. It is not enough to declare the
book finished. The current release machinery validates release-record shape,
audience scope, derived-artifact boundaries, scaffold/proof sync, and
publication hygiene. It leaves manuscript quality, source accuracy, claim
truth, external review, reader-release approval, and audio production open
unless separate records say otherwise.

The next practical object is a more explicit change-packet record. It should
make one chapter edit, one outline update, one proof-manifest shift, one
reader-generation event, or one blocked release easy to inspect as a single
auditable unit. That would reduce dependence on conversational memory and make
future autonomous writing runs less ambiguous.

## Beyond The State Of The Art

The mature endpoint is a research operating system that renders as a book.

In that endpoint, a future agent can receive a new AI paper, decide whether it
belongs in an existing chapter or a new one, add public-safe source metadata,
write a source note, update the outline, adjust proof and test backlogs, draft
the relevant prose, update claim/evidence records, run validation, render the
site, and prepare reader/audiobook derivatives without hand-renumbering or
hidden claim promotion.

The live site remains the canonical AI/research surface. Human view gives
interested readers a cleaner on-site path. Major-version reader editions,
ebooks, PDFs, DOCX files, and audio scripts are downstream projections with
release records and review gates. A target format is not an artifact. A
generated artifact is not an approved artifact. An approved reader artifact is
not evidence that the ASI Stack works.

Compared with literate programming and executable-book practice, the mature
endpoint is claim-state aware. It would not only weave explanation with
artifacts; it would preserve source readiness, support state, proof scope,
failed validation, review state, release approval, reader-edition deltas, and
non-claims as first-class publication objects.

At full maturity, the living-book system would also be able to lose cleanly. A
source could contradict a chapter. A proof attempt could fail. A benchmark lane
could demote a claim. A consolidation review could merge chapters or reject a
proposed merge. An external reviewer could say the contribution is not novel
or is already handled by prior work. The book would preserve that result
instead of smoothing it away.

That is the real ambition of the methodology: not continuous praise, but
continuous accountability.

## What This Chapter Does Not Prove

This reader chapter does not approve a reader release. It does not create an
EPUB, PDF, DOCX, audiobook, or audio-embedded edition. It does not prove source
interpretation quality, manuscript quality, rendered-site availability,
external-review adequacy, or ASI capability. It does not move the chapter core
claim above `argument`.

It records a human-reader prose pass over the methodology. The live AI/research
chapter, Appendix C, proof manifest, release records, and validation outputs
remain the authority for claims and evidence.

## Summary

Living Book Methodology is the book's self-governance layer. It says that the
manuscript should improve the way the architecture says intelligence should
improve: through stable identity, source discipline, claim states, proof and
test hooks, release records, residuals, and visible non-claims.

Paired with Evidence States, it is the book's methodological contribution: a
way for a technical manuscript to keep moving without letting confidence,
citations, proofs, tests, and reader polish drift apart.

The point is not bureaucracy. The point is memory. A future reader, reviewer,
or AI assistant should be able to resume from the repository state rather than
from private recollection. If the repo cannot explain what changed, why it
changed, what ran, what failed, what is evidenced, and what remains blocked,
the living-book layer is not alive enough.

## Handoff

A living methodology needs a managed backlog. Open Research Agenda and
Bibliography Plan supplies that control surface: what still needs to be read,
mined, compared, tested, proved, imported, reviewed, merged, deferred, or
rejected. It keeps future additions from turning the architecture back into a
pile of papers.
